questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
The general solution of the differential equation (y<sup>2</sup>
– x<sup>3</sup>)dx – xydy = 0 (x $$ \ne $$ 0) is :
(where c is a constant of integration)
questions.solution:
$$({y^2} - {x^3})dx - xydy = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow y(ydx - xdy) = {x^3}dx$$

$$ \Rightarrow {y \over x}\left( {{{ydx - xdy} \over {{x^2}}}} \right) = dx$$

$$ \Rightarrow - {y \over x}d\left( {{y \over x}} \right) = dx$$

$$ \Rightarrow - {1 \over 2}{\left( {{y \over x}} \right)^2} = x + k$$

$$ \Rightarrow - {y^2} = 2{x^3} + 2{x^2}k$$

$$ \Rightarrow {y^2} + 2{x^3} + c{x^2} = 0$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If $$y=y(x)$$ is the solution of the differential equation $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+2 y=\sin (2 x), y(0)=\frac{3}{4}$$, then $$y\left(\frac{\pi}{8}\right)$$ is equal to :
questions.solution: $$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=\sin 2 x \\ & \text { IF }=e^{2 d x}=e^{2 x} \\ & y \cdot e^{2 x}=\int e^{2 x} \sin 2 x d x+c \\ & =\frac{e^{2 x}}{8}(2 \sin 2 x-2 \cos 2 x)+c \\ & y(0)=\frac{3}{4} \\ & \frac{3}{4}=\frac{1}{8}(-2)+c \Rightarrow c=1 \\ & \text { Put } x=\frac{\pi}{8} \\ & y=\frac{1}{8} \times 2\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+e^{-\pi / 4} \\ & y=e^{-\pi / 4} \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $$y'\left( x \right) + y\left( x \right)g'\left( x \right) = g\left( x \right),g'\left( x \right),y\left( 0 \right) = 0,x \in R,$$ where $$f'(x)$$ denotes $${{df\left( x \right)} \over {dx}}$$ and $$g(x)$$ is a given non-constant differentiable function on $$R$$ with $$g(0)=g(2)=0.$$ Then the value  of $$y(2)$$ is
questions.solution:
It is given that

$$y'(x) + y(x)g'(x) = g(x)g'(x)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {e^{g(x)}}y'(x) + {e^{g(x)}}g'(x)y(x) = {e^{g(x)}}g(x)g'(x)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {d \over {dx}}(y(x){e^{g(x)}} )= {e^{g(x)}}g(x)g'(x)$$

Therefore, $$y(x) = {e^{g(x)}} = \int {{e^{g(x)}}g(x)g'(x)dx} $$

$$ = \int {{e^t}t\,dt} $$ [where g(x) = t]

$$ = (t - 1){e^t} + c$$

Therefore, $$y(x){e^{g(x)}} = (g(x) - 1){e^{g(x)}} + c$$

Substituting $$x = 0 \Rightarrow 0 = (0 - 1) \times 1 + c \Rightarrow c = 1$$

Substituting $$x = 2 \Rightarrow y(2) \times 1 = (0 - 1) \times (1) + 1$$

Hence, $$y(2) = 0$$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The differential equation representing the family of ellipse having foci eith on the x-axis or on the $$y$$-axis, center at the origin and passing through the point (0, 3) is :
questions.solution:
Equation of ellipse,

$${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$

As ellipse passes through (0, 3)

$$\therefore\,\,\,$$ $${{{0^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{3^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$    b2 = 9

$$\therefore\,\,\,$$ Equation of ellipse becomes,

$${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over 9} = 1$$

Differentiating w.r.t    x, we get,

$${{2x} \over {a{}^2}}$$ + $${{2y} \over 9}$$ . $${{dy} \over {dx}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$    $${x \over {{a^2}}}$$ = $$-$$ $${y \over 9}.{{dy} \over {da}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$    $${x \over {{a^2}}} = - {y \over 9}.y'......$$ (1)

We got earlier,

$${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over 9}$$ = 1

$$ \Rightarrow $$   $${x \over {{a^2}}}.x + {{{y^2}} \over 9} = 1$$

putting value of equation (1) here,

$$ {-{y\,y'} \over 9}.x + {{{y^2}} \over 9} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$    $$-$$ xyy' + y2 = 9

$$ \Rightarrow $$   xyy' $$-$$ y2 + 9 = 0

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: What are the order and the degree respectively of the differential equation $\rm x^2\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)^2+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^4+\sin x=0$ ?
questions.solution:
Given: $ \rm x^2\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)^2+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^4+\sin x=0 $

Concept:

Order: The order of a differential equation is determined by the highest order derivative that appears in the equation.

Degree: The degree of a differential equation is the exponent of the highest order derivative when the equation is free from radicals and any fractional powers with respect to the derivatives.

Calculation:

$ \rm x^2\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)^2+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^4+\sin x=0 $

The highest derivative in the equation is $\rm \frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}$, making the order 3.

$ \rm x^2\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)^2+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^4+\sin x=0 $

The degree is determined by the power of this highest derivative, which is 2.

Hence, the correct answer is option D.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Let y = y(x) be the solution of the differential equation,

$${({x^2} + 1)^2}{{dy} \over {dx}} + 2x({x^2} + 1)y = 1$$

such that y(0) = 0. If $$\sqrt ay(1)$$ = $$\pi \over 32$$ , then the value of 'a' is :
questions.solution:
$${({x^2} + 1)^2}{{dy} \over {dx}} + 2x({x^2} + 1)y = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${{dy} \over {dx}} + \left( {{{2x} \over {1 + {x^2}}}} \right)y = {1 \over {{{\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)}^2}}}$$

I.F = $${e^{\int {{{2x} \over {1 + {x^2}}}dx} }} = {e^{\ln \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)}} = 1 + {x^2}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${d \over {dx}}\left( {\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)y} \right) = {1 \over {1 + {x^2}}}$$

Integrating both sides we get,

$${\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)y}$$ = $${\tan ^{ - 1}}x$$ + C

When x = 0 then y = 0

So, 0 = 0 + C

$$ \Rightarrow $$ C = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)y}$$ = $${\tan ^{ - 1}}x$$

Put x = 1,

y.(2) = $${\pi \over 4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ y = $${\pi \over 8}$$ = y(1)

Given,

$$\sqrt ay(1)$$ = $$\pi \over 32$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\sqrt a. {\pi \over 8}$$ = $$\pi \over 32$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\sqrt a = {1 \over 4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$a = {1 \over {16}}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $\left(x^2-x \sqrt{x^2-1}\right) d y+\left(y\left(x-\sqrt{x^2-1}\right)-x\right) d x=0, x \geq 1$. If $y(1)=1$, then the greatest integer less than $y(\sqrt{5})$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.solution:
First write the given differential equation in the standard linear form.

$$\frac{dy}{dx}+\frac{y}{x}=\frac{1}{x-\sqrt{x^2-1}}$$

Now simplify the right side by rationalising:

$$\frac{1}{x-\sqrt{x^2-1}}=\frac{x+\sqrt{x^2-1}}{(x-\sqrt{x^2-1})(x+\sqrt{x^2-1})}=\frac{x+\sqrt{x^2-1}}{x^2-(x^2-1)}=x+\sqrt{x^2-1}$$

So the equation becomes:

$$\frac{dy}{dx}+\frac{y}{x}=x+\sqrt{x^2-1}$$

This is a linear differential equation with integrating factor (I.F.):

I.f $= e^{\ln x}=x$

Multiply the whole equation by $x$:

$$y.x=\int \left(x+\sqrt{x^2-1}\right)x\,dx$$

Now integrate term by term:

$$=\frac{x^2}{2}+\frac{\left(x^2-1\right)^{3/2}}{3}+C$$

Use the initial condition $y(1)=1$ to find $C$.

Given $y(1)=1$

Substitute $x=1$ in the expression for $yx$:

$$1=\frac{1}{2}+C \rightarrow C=\frac{1}{2}$$

So,

$$yx=\frac{x^2}{2}+\frac{(x^2-1)^{3/2}}{3}+\frac{1}{2}$$

Divide by $x$:

$$y=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{(x^2-1)^{3/2}}{3x}+\frac{1}{2x}$$

Now put $x=\sqrt{5}$:

$$\left[y\left(\sqrt{5}\right)\right]=\left[\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}+\frac{8}{3}+\frac{1}{2\sqrt{5}}\right]=3$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Match the statements/expressions in Column I with the values given in Column II:

Column I Column II
(A) The number of solutions of the equation $$x{e^{\sin x}} - \cos x = 0$$ in the interval $$\left( {0,{\pi \over 2}} \right)$$ (P) 1
(B) Value(s) of $$k$$ for which the planes $$kx + 4y + z = 0,4x + ky + 2z = 0$$ and $$2x + 2y + z = 0$$ intersect in a straight line (Q) 2
(C) Value(s) of $$k$$ for which $$|x - 1| + |x - 2| + |x + 1| + |x + 2| = 4k$$ has integer solution(s) (R) 3
(D) If $$y' = y + 1$$ and $$y(0) = 1$$ then value(s) of $$y(\ln 2)$$ (S) 4
(T) 5
questions.solution:
(A) We have $$f'(x) > 0,\forall x \in (0,\pi /2)$$. Therefore,

$$f(0) < 0$$ and $$f(\pi /2) > 0$$

Hence, there is no one solution.

(B) Let us consider that $$(a,b,c)$$ is direction ratio of the intersected line. Therefore,

$$ak + 4b + c = 0$$

$$4a + kb + 2c = 0$$

$${a \over {8 - k}} = {b \over {4 - 2k}} = {c \over {{k^2} - 16}}$$

We need to have

$$2(8 - k) + 2(4 - 2k) + ({k^2} - 16) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow k = 2,4$$.

(C) Let us consider

$$f(x) = |x + 2| + |x + 1| + |x - 1| + |x - 2|$$

Therefore, $$k$$ can take values: 2, 3, 4, 5.

(D) $$\int {{{dy} \over {y + 1}} = \int {dx} } $$

$$ \Rightarrow f(x) = 2{e^x} - 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow f(\ln 2) = 3$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $x=x(\mathrm{t})$ and $y=y(\mathrm{t})$ be solutions of the differential equations $\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}+\mathrm{a} x=0$ and $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}+\mathrm{by}=0$ respectively, $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \in \mathbf{R}$. Given that $x(0)=2 ; y(0)=1$ and $3 y(1)=2 x(1)$, the value of $\mathrm{t}$, for which $x(\mathrm{t})=y(\mathrm{t})$, is :
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}+\mathrm{ax}=0 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}=-\mathrm{adt} \\ & \int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}=-\mathrm{a} \int \mathrm{dt} \\ & \ln |\mathrm{x}|=-\mathrm{at}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \mathrm{at} \mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{x}=2 \\ & \ln 2=0+\mathrm{c} \\ & \ln \mathrm{x}=-\mathrm{at}+\ln 2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}=\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{at}} \\ & \mathrm{x}=2 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{at}} \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d t}+b y=0 \\ & \frac{d y}{y}=-b d t \\ & \ln |y|=-b t+\lambda \\ & t=0, y=1 \\ & 0=0+\lambda \\ & y=e^{-b t} \quad \text{..... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

According to question

$$\begin{aligned} & 3 \mathrm{y}(1)=2 \mathrm{x}(1) \\ & 3 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{b}}=2\left(2 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{a}}\right) \\ & \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}}=\frac{4}{3} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { For } x(t)=y(t) \\ & \begin{array}{r} 2 \mathrm{e}^{-a t}=e^{-b t} \\ 2=e^{(a-b) t} \\ 2=\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^t \\ \log _{\frac{4}{3}} 2=t \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Suppose the solution of the differential equation $$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{(2+\alpha) x-\beta y+2}{\beta x-2 \alpha y-(\beta \gamma-4 \alpha)}$$ represents a circle passing through origin. Then the radius of this circle is :
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{(2+\alpha) x-\beta y+2}{\beta x-2 \alpha y-(\beta \gamma-4 \alpha)} \\ & \beta x d y-2 \alpha y d y-(\beta \gamma-4 \alpha) d y \\ & =2 x d x+\alpha x d x-\beta y d x+2 d x \\ & \beta \int(x d y+y d y)-\alpha y^2-(\beta \gamma-4 x) y=x^2+\frac{\alpha x^2}{2}+2 x \\ & \beta x y-\alpha y^2-(\beta \gamma-4 \alpha) y=x^2+\frac{\alpha x^2}{2}+2 x \\ & \left(1+\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) x^2+\alpha y^2-\beta x y+2 x+(\beta \gamma-4 \alpha) y=0 \\ & \because \text { this represents circle passing through origin } \\ & \Rightarrow \beta=0 \text { and } 1+\frac{\alpha}{2}=\alpha \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore C: 2 x^2+2 y^2+2 x-8 y=0 \\ & x^2+y^2+x-4 y=0 \\ & \text { Radius }=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}+4-0} \\ & \quad=\frac{\sqrt{17}}{2} \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Let $y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation

$$ x^2 \frac{d y}{d x}+x y=x^2+y^2, \quad x>\frac{1}{e} $$

satisfying $y(1)=0$. Then the value of $2 \frac{(y(e))^2}{y\left(e^2\right)}$ is ____________.
questions.solution:
$\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}=\frac{x^2}{x^2}+\frac{y^2}{x^2}$

$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}=1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2 \\ & \text { Let } \frac{y}{x}=t \\ & y=x t \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}=x \frac{d t}{d x}+t \\ & \therefore x \frac{d t}{d x}+t+t=1+t^2 \\ & \Rightarrow x \frac{d t}{d x}+2 t=1+t^2 \\ & \Rightarrow x \frac{d t}{d x}+t^2+1-2 t \\ & \Rightarrow x \frac{d t}{d x}=(t-1)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d t}{(t-1)^2}=\frac{d x}{x}\end{aligned}$

Integrating both sides

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \int \frac{d t}{(t-1)^2}=\int \frac{d x}{x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-1}{(t-1)}=\ln x+C \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-1}{\frac{y}{x}-1}=\ln x+C \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-x}{y-x}=\ln x+C \end{aligned} $$

Given $y(1)=0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & 1=C \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-x}{y-x}=\ln x+1 ..........(i)\end{aligned} $$

$\therefore $ Put $x=e$

$\begin{aligned} & \frac{-e}{y-e}=1+1 \\ & \Rightarrow-e=2(y-e) \\ & \Rightarrow e=2(e-y) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{e}{2}=e-y \\ & \Rightarrow y=e-\frac{e}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{e}{2} \Rightarrow y=\frac{e}{2} \\ & \text { Put } x=e^2 \text { in (i) } \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-e^2}{y-e^2}=2+1\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow-e^2=3\left(y-e^2\right) \\ & \Rightarrow-e^2=3 y-3 e^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 e^2=3 y \\ & \Rightarrow y=\frac{2}{3} e^2 \\ & \therefore \frac{2(y(e))^2}{y\left(e^2\right)}=2 \frac{\left(\frac{e}{2}\right)^2}{\frac{2}{3} e^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{2}{3}}=\frac{3}{4}=00.75\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
The solution curve of the differential equation,

(1 + e-x)(1 + y2)$${{dy} \over {dx}}$$ = y2,

which passes through the point (0, 1), is :
questions.solution:
Given (1 + e-x)(1 + y2)$${{dy} \over {dx}}$$ = y2

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\left( {{{{y^2} + 1} \over {{y^2}}}} \right)dy = {{{e^x}dx} \over {{e^x} + 1}}$$

Integrating both sides,

$$\int {\left( {{{{y^2} + 1} \over {{y^2}}}} \right)dy = } \int {{{{e^x}dx} \over {{e^x} + 1}}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$y - {1 \over y} = ln\left| {{e^x} + 1} \right| + c$$

It passes through (0, 1)

$$ \Rightarrow $$ c = $$ - ln2$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${y^2} - 1 = yln\left( {{{{e^x} + 1} \over 2}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${y^2} = 1 + yln\left( {{{{e^x} + 1} \over 2}} \right)$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
If $$y = y(x)$$ is the solution of the differential equation

$$x{{dy} \over {dx}} + 2y = x\,{e^x}$$, $$y(1) = 0$$ then the local maximum value

of the function $$z(x) = {x^2}y(x) - {e^x},\,x \in R$$ is :

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $x \sqrt{1-x^2} d y+\left(y \sqrt{1-x^2}-x \cos ^{-1} x\right) d x=0, x \in(0,1), \lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} y(x)=1$. Then $y\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)$ equals :
questions.solution:
$\left(x \sqrt{1-x^2}\right) d y+\left(y \sqrt{1-x^2}-x \cos ^{-1} x\right) d x=0$

$\Rightarrow\left(x \sqrt{1-x^2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(y \sqrt{1-x^2}-x \cos ^{-1} x\right)=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{\left(y \sqrt{1-x^2}-x \cos ^{-1} x\right)}{\left(x \sqrt{1-x^2}\right)}=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}=\frac{\cos ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \ldots . $$(i)

Bernoulli's linear ODE of first order :

$\frac{d y}{d x}+P(x) \cdot y=Q(x)$ having $\boldsymbol{I} \cdot \boldsymbol{F}=\boldsymbol{e}^{\int \boldsymbol{P}(\boldsymbol{x}) \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{x}}$

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \boldsymbol{P}(\boldsymbol{x}) & \boldsymbol{Q}(\boldsymbol{x}) \\ \hline \frac{1}{\boldsymbol{x}} & \frac{\cos ^{-1} \boldsymbol{x}}{\sqrt{1-\boldsymbol{x}^2}} \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$ \Rightarrow I . F=e^{\int P(x) d x}=e^{\int \frac{d x}{x}}=e^{\ln x}=x \ldots . . (ii)$$

Apply the I.F from equation $(i i)$ in $\mathrm{ODE}(i)$ :

$$ \text { - } x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{x \cos ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \Rightarrow x d y+y d x=\left(\frac{x \cos ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) d x $$

$$ \Rightarrow d(x y)=\left(\frac{x \cos ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) d x \Rightarrow \int d(x y)=\int\left(\frac{x \cos ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) d x \ldots . .(iii) $$

We choose the first and second functions

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \boldsymbol{u} & \boldsymbol{v} \\ \hline \cos ^{-1} \boldsymbol{x} & \frac{\boldsymbol{x}}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \\ \hline \end{array}\\ &\text { for the integrand in equation }(\boldsymbol{i} \boldsymbol{i} \boldsymbol{i}) \text { to proceed further: } \end{aligned} $$

$\begin{aligned} x y & =\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \int\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) d x+\int\left[\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) \cdot \int\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) d x\right] d x \\ & =-\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \sqrt{1-x^2}-\int\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) \cdot\left(\sqrt{1-x^2}\right) d x \\ & =-\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \sqrt{1-x^2}-\int\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) \cdot\left(\sqrt{1-x^2}\right) d x \\ & =-\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \sqrt{1-x^2}-\int d x \\ & =-\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \sqrt{1-x^2}-x+C\end{aligned}$

$y(x)=\frac{C}{x}-1-\frac{\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \sqrt{1-x^2}}{x} \ldots .(i v)$ where $C$ is the arbitrary constant

$\lim\limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} y(x)=\lim\limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}}\left[\frac{C}{x}-1-\frac{\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \sqrt{1-x^2}}{x}\right]=C-1-\frac{(0) \cdot(0)}{1}=C-1 \ldots \ldots(v)$

We have the boundary condition

$\lim\limits_{\boldsymbol{x} \rightarrow 1^{-}} \boldsymbol{y}(\boldsymbol{x})=1 \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{C}=2 \ldots . .(\boldsymbol{v} \boldsymbol{i}) \leftarrow$ From equation $(\boldsymbol{v})$

We have solved the ODE to reach a particular solution $(\boldsymbol{C}=2)$ from $(\boldsymbol{i v}) y(x)=\frac{2}{x}-1-\frac{\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \sqrt{1-x^2}}{x} \ldots$. (vii)

Substitute $\boldsymbol{x}=\frac{1}{2}$ in equation (vii) to evaluate $\boldsymbol{y}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=4-1-(\boldsymbol{\mathcal { 2 }}) \cdot\left(\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{3}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\boldsymbol{\mathcal { 2 }}}\right)$

$=3-\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{3}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The order of the differential equation $\frac{d^4 y}{d x^4}-\sin \left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)=5$
questions.solution:
Highest order derivative present in the given differential equation is 4 .

Thus, order of differential equation $=4$
